summary: Spolsky draws an analogy between manufacturing inventory and software development, arguing that accumulated work-in-progress — feature backlogs, bug databases, and undeployed features — represents costly inventory that slows teams down. He identifies three key accumulation points where software 'inventory' piles up and provides concrete suggestions for constraining each. The post argues that excessive backlogs waste time on features that will never ship, bloated bug databases divert effort from the product itself, and undeployed features forfeit competitive advantage. He connects this thinking to the origin of Trello, which was designed to make inventory visible and discourage accumulation. The core message is that lean principles from manufacturing apply directly to software teams, and constraining inventory at every stage is essential for shipping faster.
key_points: Software development has inventory accumulation points analogous to manufacturing: feature backlogs, bug databases, and undeployed code all represent costly work-in-progress.
90% of feature backlog items will never be implemented, so time spent grooming, speccing, or discussing them is largely wasted.
Bug databases tend toward bankruptcy — thousands of entries that represent months of wasted effort on reports nobody will fix.
Undeployed features are the most expensive inventory because they could be earning money but instead sit idle while competitors ship.
Making inventory visible (as Trello does) helps teams identify bottlenecks and resist the urge to accumulate more work than they can ship.
money_quotes: 90% of the things in the feature backlog will never get implemented, ever.
The backlog, in fact, should be seen as a list of things you are not allowed to talk about or work on.

When I hear about product teams that regularly have 'backlog grooming' sessions, in which they carefully waste a tiny amount of time and mental energy every day or every week thinking about every single feature which will never be implemented, I want to poke my eyes out.
At some point you realize that you've put too much work into the bug database and not quite enough work into the product.
Don't worry, the severe bugs will come back.
This is one of the most expensive forms of inventory: unshipped feature inventory. It could be earning you money, but it's sitting on the shipping dock of your factory.
The 'cost' of code inventory is huge. It might add up to six or twelve months of work that is stuck in the assembly line and not yet in customers' hands.
key_insight: Software teams should treat accumulated backlogs, bug databases, and undeployed features as costly inventory to be ruthlessly constrained, not carefully maintained.
